deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers—no rationale is given for the Socratic approach in terms of learning benefits, retention, or skill development.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turn 10; Transcript 2, Turn 10; Transcript 3, Turn 10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful progress (e.g., arriving at the means/side-effect distinction or drafting a rule), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice: it never gives away answers, consistently redirects students to test their own ideas, and scaffolds well from simple to complex. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach and a missed opportunity to prompt reflection after student breakthroughs. The 1.3.B criterion is borderline—the tutor does give some process-level feedback (e.g., 'stop listing labels and test them'), so no deduction was applied there. The 1.3.C deduction is applied because in all three transcripts the tutor moves directly to the next challenge after student progress without pausing to consolidate what changed.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate key ideas before confirming them—no deductions warranted. 1.2: Scaffolding is well-executed across all three transcripts; the tutor builds on student progress and uses diagnostic questions before redirecting. 1.3: Two deductions apply. First, the tutor never explains why it is using a question-led approach rather than direct answers (1.3.A.a). Second, in all three transcripts, when students make genuine progress—arriving at the means/side-effect distinction or drafting a rule—the tutor immediately pushes to the next challenge without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking (1.3.C.a). 1.3.B is not penalized because the tutor does give method-level feedback (e.g., 'don't guess from the label—reconstruct the structure'). Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong throughout: responses are concise, tone is supportive, and the tutor stays on-task.
